153	\section{Converse direction (milestone M4)}
154	\label{sec:converse-out-of-scope}
155	
156	This direction is recorded as an optional future milestone~M4 (see \texttt{STRATEGY.md}). Closure requires the deformation-theoretic hypothesis \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)}, which the project may wire up if a downstream consumer demands the iff form. The remainder of this section documents the obstruction in concrete form: a counterexample showing that without that hypothesis the converse fails, and a comparison with the Mathlib converse lemma at the ring level.
157	
158	The reverse implication
159	\[
160	  \forall x\in X,\ \exists\,U\text{ affine},\ \Omega_{X/S}(U)\text{ free of rank }n
161	  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f \text{ smooth of relative dimension } n
162	\]
163	is \emph{mathematically false} as stated, even under the auxiliary hypothesis that \(f\) is locally of finite presentation. Local freeness of \(\Omega\) alone does not imply flatness, and flatness (equivalently, formal smoothness via vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology) is genuine deformation-theoretic content not encoded in the section-module hypothesis. This is why the iff form of the criterion present in earlier iterations was demoted to a forward implication.
164	
165	\begin{remark}[Explicit counterexample]
166	  \label{rem:converse_counterexample}
167	  Let \(k\) be a field and consider the morphism \(f \colon \Spec k \to \Spec k[t]\) induced by the surjection \(k[t] \twoheadrightarrow k\) sending \(t \mapsto 0\). Then \(f\) is locally of finite presentation, its source \(\Spec k\) has a unique affine open (itself), and the K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{k/k[t]} = 0\) is free of rank \(0\) (the trivial \texttt{Module.Basis} indexed by the empty set witnesses this). Yet \(f\) is the closed immersion of a non-open point of \(\Spec k[t]\), hence is not flat and therefore not smooth, so \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(0\)~\(f\) fails. The local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) is therefore strictly weaker than smoothness, and the converse implication of the iff form fails on this very example.
168	\end{remark}
169	
170	\begin{remark}[The Mathlib converse lemma and its hypotheses]
171	  \label{rem:converse_lemma_hypotheses}
172	  Mathlib does provide a converse-direction lemma at the ring level,
173	  \[
174	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.of\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
175	  \]
176	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothOfFree}). Its three hypotheses make the missing content explicit:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{Algebra.FinitePresentation}~\(A\)~\(B\);
179	    \item \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)} --- vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology, i.e.\ formal smoothness;
180	    \item a basis \(b \colon \texttt{Module.Basis}~I~B~\Omega_{B/A}\) whose range lies in the image of the universal derivation, \(\mathrm{Set.range}\,(\texttt{Algebra.KaehlerDifferential.D}~A~B)\).
181	  \end{itemize}
182	  None of these three is supplied by the section-module hypothesis of the would-be iff. The first-cotangent-vanishing hypothesis in particular is the deformation-theoretic input that distinguishes ``\(\Omega\) locally free'' from ``smooth''; without it, the counterexample of \cref{rem:converse_counterexample} would already break the converse. Establishing \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)} from project-side hypotheses (e.g.\ via a flatness assumption added to the input) is the work that would close the converse, and is the content of milestone~M4.
183	\end{remark}
184	
185	\begin{remark}[Cross-reference to Stacks 02G1]
186	  \label{rem:stacks_02G1}
187	  The standard textbook biconditional ``\(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d\) iff \(f\) is flat, locally of finite presentation, and \(\Omega_{X/S}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)'' is Stacks Tag~02G1 (and is the Noetherian-case Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2). The flatness conjunct is precisely what is missing from a hypothesis that only asserts local freeness of \(\Omega\); supplying it amounts to the deformation-theoretic content discussed in \cref{rem:converse_lemma_hypotheses}. The forward implication proved as \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} above corresponds to the easy direction of the same tag.
188	\end{remark}
189	
190	\section{Content scheduled for later milestones}
191	
192	Each bullet below is a future milestone whose Mathlib infrastructure is not yet available in the pinned snapshot \texttt{b80f227}; inclusion in this list does \emph{not} mean the project has abandoned it. The roadmap progression is: M1 (this iter) \(\to\) M2 / M3 (existence routes) \(\to\) M4 (converse, \cref{sec:converse-out-of-scope}) \(\to\) M5+ (sheafification, cotangent exact sequence, etc., listed here). Each item lists the deferred concept, the missing Mathlib piece, and one cross-reference for forward-looking reinstatement.
193	
194	\begin{itemize}
195	  \item \textbf{Sheaf condition for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (M5).} The relative cotangent presheaf is, on an affine basis, locally isomorphic to Mathlib's quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{\Omega_{B/A}}\) on each affine chart, and the unique-gluing characterisation of the sheaf property holds basis-wise. No off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma packages ``\texttt{Scheme.PresheafOfModules}-sheaf-on-affine-basis \(\Rightarrow\) sheaf on \(X\)'' however; the cofinality descent against \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafOpensLeCover} is the deferred work for this milestone. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.5 (sheaves from affine charts) and Stacks Tag~009H.
196	
197	  \item \textbf{Cotangent exact sequence (M6).} For a composition of morphisms \(X \to Y \to S\), there is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent \(\struct{X}\)-modules
198	  \[
199	    f^{*}\Omega_{Y/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}\; \Omega_{X/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}\;
200	      \Omega_{X/Y} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
201	  \]
202	  The ring-level ingredients (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map} and the surjectivity of the right map) are in Mathlib, but the exactness conjunct requires \texttt{SheafOfModules.exact\_iff\_stalkwise} or an equivalent section-wise+sheafification route through homology-preserving instances of the forget functor; both are Mathlib gaps. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.4A and Stacks Tag~01UV.
203	
204	  \item \textbf{Cotangent space at a section (M7).} A corollary of the smoothness criterion above, pulled back along a section \(s \colon S \to X\): if \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\) and \(s\) is a section, then \(s^{*}\Omega_{X/S}\) is a locally free \(\struct{S}\)-module of rank \(n\). The natural Lean framing uses \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback} against the sheaf-bundled \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}\), which was trimmed in iter-117; a presheaf-form analogue against \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback} would need a separate formulation. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Corollary~8.10.
205	
206	  \item \textbf{Serre-duality genus identity (M8).} On a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C/k\),
207	  \[
208	    \dim_{k} H^{0}(C, \Omega_{C/k}) \;=\; \dim_{k} H^{1}(C, \struct{C}),
209	  \]
210	  both sides being equal to the genus \(g(C)\). Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf, no trace morphism for proper morphisms, and no Zariski coherent cohomology of \(\struct{X}\)-modules, so the equality cannot yet be expressed within the project's Mathlib snapshot. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~III.7 and Stacks Tag~0FVU.
211	\end{itemize}

8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
131	avoids. The
132	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
133	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
134	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
135	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
136	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
137	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
138	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
139	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
140	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
141

267	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
268	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
269	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
270	
271	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
272	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
273	is already present in Mathlib: the file
274	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
275	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
276	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
277	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
278	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
279	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
280	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
281	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
282	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
283	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
284	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
285	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
286	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
287	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
288	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
289	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
290	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
291	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
292	
293	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
294	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
295	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
296	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
297	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
298	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
299	independently of any upstream timing.

301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392

395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with

401	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
402	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
403	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,

405	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of

408	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance

411	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
412	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.

414	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
415	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
416	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
417	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
418	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
419	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
420	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
421	  fixed-base presheaves in
422	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
423	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
424	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
425	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
426	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
427	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
428	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
429	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
430	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
431	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
432	
433	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
434	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
435	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
436	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
437	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
438	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
439	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
440	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
441	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
442	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
443	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
444	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
445	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
446	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
447	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
448	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
449	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
450	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
451	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
452	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
453	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
454	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
455	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
456	\end{remark}

458	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
459	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
460	
461	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
462	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
463	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
464	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
465	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
466	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
467	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
468	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
470	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
471	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
472	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
473	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
474	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
475	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
476	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
477	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
478	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
479	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
480	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
481	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
482	to the off-path route~(e).
483	
484	\begin{lemma}
485	
486	\leanok
487	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
488	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
489	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
490	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
491	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
492	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
493	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
494	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
495	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
496	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
497	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
498	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
499	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
500	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
501	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
502	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
503	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
504	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
505	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
506	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
507	  \[
508	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
509	      \;\;:=\;\;
510	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
511	  \]
512	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
513	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
514	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
515	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
516	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
517	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
518	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
519	\end{lemma}
520	
521	\begin{proof}
522	
523	
524	\leanok
525	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
526	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
527	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
528	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
529	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
530	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
531	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
532	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
533	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
534	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
535	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
536	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
537	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
538	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
539	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
540	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
541	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
542	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
543	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
544	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a definitional
545	  transport across the unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
546	
547	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
548	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
549	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
550	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
551	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
552	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
553	\end{proof}
554	
555	\begin{lemma}
556	
557	
558	\leanok
559	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
560	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
562	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
563	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
564	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
565	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
566	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
567	  morphism
568	  \[
569	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
570	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
571	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
572	  \]
573	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
574	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
575	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
576	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
577	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
578	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
579	\end{lemma}
580	
581	\begin{proof}
582	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
583	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
584	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
585	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
586	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
587	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
588	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
589	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
590	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
591	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
592	  isomorphism}
593	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
594	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
595	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
596	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
597	  therefore extension of scalars along
598	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
599	
600	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
601	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
602	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
603	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
604	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
605	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
606	  the abstract pullback functor.
607	
608	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
609	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
610	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
611	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
612	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
613	   \;\cong\;
614	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
615	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
616	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
617	  comparison
618	  \[
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
620	      \;\longrightarrow\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
622	      \;\otimes\;
623	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
624	  \]
625	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
626	
627	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
628	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
629	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
630	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
631	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
633	  % "\begin{lemma}
634	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
635	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
636	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
637	  % \begin{enumerate}
638	  % \item The functor
639	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
640	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
641	  % \item The functor
642	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
643	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
644	  %  [\dots]
645	  % \end{enumerate}
646	  % \end{lemma}
647	  %  [\dots]
648	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
649	  % of functors, see
650	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
651	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
652	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
653	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
654	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
655	
656	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
657	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
658	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
659	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
660	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
661	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
662	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
663	
664	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of

788	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
789	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
790	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
791	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
792	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
793	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.

815	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));

819	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
820	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
821	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
822	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
823	  \end{enumerate}

827	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
828	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
829	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
830	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective

839	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
840	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
841	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of

848	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
849	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
850	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring

915	\begin{lemma}

917	\leanok
918	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
919	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
920	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.

922	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
923	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
924	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat}
925	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
926	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
927	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
928	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
929	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
930	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
931	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
932	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
933	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
934	  left-whiskered morphism
935	  \[
936	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
937	  \]
938	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
939	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
940	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
941	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
942	
943	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
944	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
945	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
946	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
947	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
948	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
949	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
950	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
951	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
952	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
953	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
954	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
955	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
956	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
957	\end{lemma}
958	
959	\begin{proof}
960	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
961	  \leanok
962	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
963	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
964	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
965	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
966	
967	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
968	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
969	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
970	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
971	  \[
972	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
973	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
974	  \]
975	
976	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
977	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
978	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
979	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
980	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
981	
982	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
983	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
984	  linear maps (Mathlib
985	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
986	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
987	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
988	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
989	
990	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
991	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
992	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
993	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
994	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
995	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
996	\end{proof}

1034	\end{lemma}

1054	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is a closed thin wrapper, retained

1058	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
1059	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
1060	
1061	\emph{Full-monoidal-category construction --- off path, not to be formalized.} The
1062	construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on all of
1063	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1064	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1065	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1066	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1067	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1068	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1069	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1070	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator is obtained as the sheafification
1071	transport of the presheaf associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), whose
1072	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation is closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
1073	commutation (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,%
1074	lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); it uses \emph{no} \emph{full}
1075	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1076	monoidal category. Accordingly the \emph{full monoidal-category} target of this
1077	section --- the \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W})
1078	and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) --- is off
1079	path and must not be re-attempted; it is kept for the historical record. The d.1/d.2
1080	stalk apparatus it shares with the live single whisker obligation is \emph{not}
1081	vestigial, however: it is on the critical path through
1082	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1083	
1084	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1085	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1086	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1087	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1088	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1089	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1090	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1091	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1092	
1093	\paragraph{What the group law consumes.} The group law is assembled directly on
1094	isomorphism classes of \emph{locally trivial} (invertible) sheaves
1095	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard
1096	group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1097	(\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). It needs only the \emph{existence} of
1098	the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1099	and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, together with \(\otimes\)-invertibility
1100	supplying inverses; it uses \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance, \emph{no}
1101	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\).
1102	It does, however, consume the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, which is
1103	realized as the sheafification transport of the presheaf associator and whose
1104	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1105	is closed by the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}).
1106	Hence the stalk ingredients (d.1)/(d.2) are \emph{on} the critical path of the group
1107	law --- through the associator's single whisker obligation --- even though the full
1108	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of
1109	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) (the subject of the present section) is \emph{not} built:
1110	that full monoidal generality, with its \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields
1111	(\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) and all derived coherence, is what the group law does not
1112	consume. The single open obligation of the present section's full-monoidal target is
1113	thus the local-injectivity half packaged for an arbitrary whisker object
1114	(\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), itself a consequence of the same d.2
1115	commutation.
1116	
1117	The following lemma is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk-map packaging (the ``d.1''
1118	ingredient). It is consumed by the stalk--tensor commutation of
1119	\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation,
1120	lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}) and is therefore a live ingredient
1121	of the unconditional associator route.

1123	\begin{lemma}
1124	
1125	\leanok
1126	% Live ingredient: consumed by lem:stalk_tensor_commutation and
1127	% lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk (sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor).

1129	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1130	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1131	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap}
1132	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1133	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1134	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1135	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1136	  \[
1137	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1138	  \]
1139	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1140	  characterised on germs by
1141	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1142	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1143	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1144	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1145	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1146	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1147	
1148	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1149	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1150	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of
1151	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} and
1152	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. They build on the
1153	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1154	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1155	\end{lemma}
1156	
1157	\begin{proof}
1158	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1159	  \leanok
1160	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1161	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1162	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1163	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1164	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1165	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1166	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1167	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1168	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1169	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1170	\end{proof}

1190	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1191	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1192	  % \lean: this superseded, off-critical-path obligation is the SAME statement
1193	  % (left-whiskering of a J.W-morphism by an arbitrary F is locally injective) as
1194	  % the LIVE lemma lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk, and is realised by
1195	  % the same sorry-free decl. Both blueprint nodes legitimately share the pin
1196	  % (leandag flags only Lean-side name collisions, not shared blueprint pins).
1197	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
1198	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1199	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1200	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1201	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1202	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1203	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1204	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1205	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1206	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1207	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1208	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1209	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1210	  left-whiskered morphism
1211	  \[
1212	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1213	  \]
1214	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1215	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1216	
1217	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1218	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1219	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1220	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1221	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1222	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1223	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1224	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1225	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1226	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1227	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1228	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1233	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1234	
1235	
1236	\leanok
1237	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1238	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1239	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1240	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1241	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1242	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1243	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1244	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1245	  On such a \(V\):
1246	  \begin{itemize}
1247	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1248	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1249	      \[
1250	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1251	      \]
1252	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1253	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1254	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1255	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1256	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1257	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1258	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1259	  \end{itemize}
1260	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1261	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1262	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1263	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1264	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1265	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1266	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1267	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1268	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1269	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1270	
1271	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1272	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1273	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1274	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1275	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1276	
1277	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1278	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1279	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1280	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1281	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1282	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1283	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1284	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1285	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1286	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1287	  \[
1288	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1289	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1290	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1291	  \]
1292	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1293	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1294	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1295	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1296	
1297	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1298	  pinned commit:
1299	  \begin{enumerate}
1300	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1301	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1302	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1303	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1304	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1305	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1306	      above.
1307	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1308	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1309	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1310	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1311	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1312	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1313	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1314	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1315	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1316	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1317	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1318	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1319	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1320	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1321	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1322	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1323	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1324	  \end{enumerate}
1325	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1326	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1327	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1328	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1329	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1330	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)

1332	
1333	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1334	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the

1350	\begin{lemma}
1351	
1352	\leanok
1353	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1354	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1355	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1356	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W}
1357	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1358	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1359	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1360	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1361	  \(J.W\):
1362	  \[
1363	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1364	    \qquad
1365	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1366	  \]
1367	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1368	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1369	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1370	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1371	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1372	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1373	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{proof}
1377	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1378	  \leanok
1379	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1380	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1381	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1382	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1383	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1384	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1385	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1386	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1387	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1388	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72
