1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
19	  
20	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
21	  \[
22	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
23	  \]
24	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
25	\end{definition}
26	
27	\begin{proof}
28	  
29	  \leanok
30	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
31	
32	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
33	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
34	    \[
35	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
36	    \]
37	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
38	  \end{remark}
39	
40	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
41	\end{proof}
42	
43	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
44	\begin{itemize}
45	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
46	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
47	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
48	\end{itemize}
49	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
50	
51	\section{Mathlib gap}
52	\label{sec:Genus_status}
53	
54	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
58	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
59	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
60	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
61	\end{itemize}
62	
63	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
64	
65	\begin{itemize}
66	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
67	\end{itemize}
68	
69	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
70	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
71	
72	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
73	
74	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
75	
76	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
77

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean
2	
3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]

6337	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
6338	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
6339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
6340	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
6341	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
6342	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
6343	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
6344	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

6355	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
6356	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
6357	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
6358	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
6359	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
6360	  \[
6361	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
6362	  \]
6363	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
6364	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
6365	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
6366	\end{lemma}
6367	
6368	\begin{proof}
6369	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv,
6370	        lem:slice_dual_transport}
6371	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
6372	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
6373	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
6374	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
6375	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
6376	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
6377	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
6378	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
6379	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
6380	
6381	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
6382	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
6383	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
6384	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
6385	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
6386	  \[
6387	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
6388	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
6389	  \]
6390	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
6391	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
6392	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
6393	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
6394	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
6395	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
6396	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
6397	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
6398	
6399	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
6400	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
6401	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
6402	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
6403	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
6404	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
6405	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
6406	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
6407	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
6408	  \emph{exactly}
6409	  \[
6410	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
6411	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
6412	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
6413	  \]
6414	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
6415	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
6416	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
6417	  two-leg split of the whole step.
6418	
6419	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
6420	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
6421	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
6422	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
6423	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
6424	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
6425	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
6426	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
6427	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
6428	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
6429	
6430	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
6431	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
6432	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
6433	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
6434	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
6435	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
6436	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
6437	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
6438	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
6439	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
6440	
6441	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
6442	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
6443	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), built
6444	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
6445	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
6446	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
6447	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
6448	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
6449	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
6450	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
6451	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The atom must
6452	  therefore be constructed directly.
6453	
6454	  Concretely (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), the forward component of
6455	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
6456	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section
6457	  component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
6458	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
6459	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).

6462	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
6463	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
6464	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
6465	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
6466	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
6467	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.

6470	  the family \(V \mapsto \mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) --- which already packages
6471	  \emph{both} legs (A) and (B) --- with the outer naturality square thin-poset-trivial. No
6472	  separate leg-(B) step is interposed in \(\mathtt{isoMk}\): the per-section atom of
6473	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content. The outer
6474	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) naturality square follows from the per-section family naturality
6475	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport.naturality}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}: the
6476	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality is established first at the level of individual sections, and the
6477	  outer presheaf-morphism naturality of \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) then follows.
6478	
6479	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
6480	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
6481	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
6482	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
6483	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
6484	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
6485	
6486	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
6487	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
6488	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
6489	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
6490	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
6491	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
6492	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
6493	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
6494	
6495	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
6496	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
6497	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
6498	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
6499	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
6500	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
6501	
6502	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
6503	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
6504	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
6505	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
6506	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
6507	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
6508	
6509	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
6510	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
6511	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
6512	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
6513	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
6514	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
6515	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
6516	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
6517	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
6518	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
6519	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
6520	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
6521	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
6522	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
6523	\end{proof}
6524	
6525	\begin{lemma}\leanok
6526	[Dual of the structure sheaf]
6527	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
6528	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
6529	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
6530	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
6531	  structure sheaf itself,
6532	  \[
6533	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
6534	  \]
6535	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
6536	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
6537	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
6538	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
6539	\end{lemma}
6540	
6541	\begin{proof}
6542	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
6543	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
6544	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
6545	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
6546	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
6547	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
6548	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
6549	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
6550	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
6551	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
6552	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
6553	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). The scheme-level alias of this

\chapter{The Quot scheme}
\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the
% functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) with
% Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
%
% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
% candidate for promotion to its own chapter
% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes
% for Plan Agent" in the writer report.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:quot_setup}

Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
===

% NOTE: Weil-divisor API extension of RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex --- re-uses
% Scheme.WeilDivisor, Scheme.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order from
% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The order map
% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order} is not re-defined here;
% see \cref{def:order_at_point} for the canonical reference.

\chapter{Codimension-1 indeterminacy extension (A.4.a)}
\label{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.a sub-row of Route A
% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). Per
% STRATEGY.md L29 it is the \emph{risk-dominant} A.4 sub-row
% ("project-fatal if it stalls"). The chapter has two outputs:
% (i) \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} --- Milne Theorem~3.1: a rational map
%     from a normal variety to a complete variety is defined on the complement
%     of a codimension-\(\geq 2\) closed subset. Specialised below to the
%     smooth-variety setting where the prover can phrase it as
%     "no codim-1 indeterminacy \(\Rightarrow\) extension". This is one of the
%     two inputs to Milne Theorem~3.2 in the sibling chapter
%     \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}.
% (ii) \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} --- Milne Lemma~3.3: for a
%     rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety, the
%     indeterminacy locus is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\). This is
%     the second input to Milne Theorem~3.2; it consumes nothing from A.4.b.
% A bonus output is \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}: the Weil-divisor
% reformulation of (i) that a smooth-surface codim-2 extension is obstructed
% exactly by the order of the pulled-back coordinates of \(f\) being negative
% at a prime divisor. This is the lemma A.4.a uses to align with the project's

\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:ab_setup}

A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed

\providecommand{\tu}{\textup}

\label{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.c assembly sub-row of

% \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
% A.1.c) into the FGA representability statement for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), following
% Kleiman, ``The Picard scheme'' \S 4 (\cite{kleiman-picard}). It is a small
% \emph{assembly} chapter: every nontrivial input is a \(\) pointer to a
% sibling chapter, and the work here is to thread the inputs through
% Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite[\S 4, Main Theorem
% th:main]{kleiman-picard}) and its specialisation to a complete variety over a
% field (\cite[\S 4, Cor.~cor:algsch]{kleiman-picard}). Identity-component
% material (\Picz) is A.3 and lives in a separate sibling chapter --- it is
% \emph{not} scoped into this chapter.

\chapter{Relative Spec}
\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:relspec_setup}

The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
organised around the relative Picard functor
\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for

\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
% references/stacks-coherent.md).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
out of scope here.

Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
\[
  \begin{array}{ccc}
    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
===

\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean

% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
% references/stacks-coherent.md).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.

We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
